Solving the Urgent Care Staffing Crisis with Smarter Credentialing

Agree & Join LinkedIn

By clicking Continue to join or sign in, you agree to LinkedIn’s User Agreement, Privacy Policy, and Cookie Policy.

Urgent care centers across the country are grappling with a staffing crisis. High patient demand, combined with clinician shortages and burnout, has left many clinics scrambling to recruit and retain qualified providers. In this climate, delays in credentialing and onboarding new staff exacerbate the problem – every day a hire isn’t cleared to work is a day the clinic remains short-staffed. This blog explores the workforce challenges urgent care providers face and how a smarter approach to credentialing, leveraging automation through platforms like Credentially, can help solve them.

### Urgent Care’s Workforce Shortage

Staffing challenges in healthcare are widespread, but urgent care has unique vulnerabilities. These clinics operate with small, “skeletal” teams – often just a couple of providers and support staff on duty – and depend on patient volume (for instance, flu season surges) to stay profitable. Labor is one of the highest costs, and when even one provider slot is vacant, it directly impacts patient throughput and revenue. Unfortunately, the COVID-19 pandemic intensified staffing issues. During the peak of COVID, urgent cares saw patient volumes skyrocket over 58% above normal in 2020, leading to extreme stress on staff. In the aftermath, many healthcare workers reached burnout. Over 230,000 medical professionals quit their positions in 2021, including physicians, nurse practitioners, and physician assistants, and an additional 117,000 clinicians left the profession entirely in that year. Alarmingly, nearly 47% of all healthcare workers plan to leave their current jobs by 2025, according to a Forbes report.

For urgent care centers, this means a shrinking pool of providers and rising turnover. Clinics often resort to temporary locum tenens providers to fill gaps, but reliance on temp staff can create a transitory environment and even impact care quality

High turnover and constant recruitment cycles are now the norm. Every urgent care operator is competing to hire from the same limited pool of clinicians, making speed in hiring and onboarding a critical advantage

### The Credentialing Bottleneck

One often-overlooked factor worsening the staffing crunch is the slow, paper-heavy process of credentialing new providers. Before a doctor, nurse practitioner, or physician assistant can start seeing patients, they must be credentialed with the urgent care’s payers and verified for licenses, certifications, and background. This process is notoriously lengthy – averaging 60 to 180 days for many healthcare organizations. In urgent care, a 2–6 month delay to onboard a provider is untenable when patient demand is immediate. Every week of delay leaves clinics understaffed, burdening existing clinicians or forcing the clinic to limit services.
title: Solving the Urgent Care Staffing Crisis with Smarter Credentialing

The Marked Shift in Urgent Care Utilization: Two Years Later

### Key Takeaways

- From 2014 to 2023, the number of urgent care centers in the U.S. increased by almost 100%, from 7,220 in 2014 to 14,382 as of the middle of 2023.
- Excluding COVID-19 visit volumes, urgent care utilization increased by 18.9% from Q2 2019 to Q2 2023 as compared to the 25.2% increase in the number of U.S. urgent care centers during the same period, suggesting that supply of urgent care clinics is outpacing demand.

You are currently viewing the public version of The Compass. To unlock additional analyses, upgrade your subscription to the Compass+ Portal.

---

Recently, we analyzed the distribution of low-acuity care across three large geographic markets, focusing on urgent care, traditional primary care providers and new entrants such as Walmart. 1 Our findings concluded that new entrants deliver less than 1% of low-acuity care, while urgent care's share has steadily increased over time in these select markets. Countless pivots within Amazon's healthcare division over the past few years, Walgreens’ rapid closure of VillageMD sites and the abrupt shuttering of all Walmart Health locations highlight the challenges of delivering low-acuity care while maintaining financial viability. 2, 3, 4

Once considered a “disruptor” in redirecting low-acuity and non-emergent care, urgent care facilities have evolved and matured to the point where many consider urgent care to be a “traditional” provider setting. Because of the dramatic change in urgent care utilization during the pandemic, we wanted to explore the sustained changes and evolution in post-pandemic consumer utilization of urgent care facilities.

### Background

While the first urgent care centers emerged in the 1970s, it took decades for the market to mature and integrate with the broader U.S. healthcare system. For many years, consumers were hesitant to visit urgent care centers because of perceptions about quality. In the last two decades, the sector has experienced significant growth, driven by the widening primary care gap and increasing consumer demand for on-demand and convenient healthcare. 5 From 2014 to 2023, the number of urgent care centers in the U.S. nearly doubled, increasing by 99.2% from 7,220 to 14,382 (Figure 1). This growth trajectory is expected to continue. 6 While there is demand for urgent care supply across all U.S. markets, the distribution of urgent care centers varies by state. Analyzing the per capita concentration of urgent care centers, urgent care is most highly concentrated in Wyoming (7.4 centers per 100K population) and least concentrated in the District of Columbia (2.4 centers per 100K population).

Experity is a Software Development company. Bitly is a company that provides an end-to-end, integrated urgent care software. Experity employs 339 people (+8.7% YoY, +37 people) and has an annual revenue of $152.2M. Headquartered in Machesney Park, Illinois, United States, operates in 5 countries (including India, Puerto Rico, Canada, and Georgia).

## About
Experity is the leading software and services company for on-demand healthcare in the U.S. urgent care market, providing an integrated operating system complete with electronic medical record, practice management, patient engagement, billing, teleradiology, business intelligence, and consulting solutions. Fifty percent of the U.S. urgent care market runs on Experity solutions. With Experity, providers can best meet the demands of the evolving on-demand space and deliver high-quality, high-velocity care by streamlining operations, improving patient experiences, and optimizing revenue. A GTCR portfolio company, Experity’s leadership is comprised of growth-minded urgent care experts and business leaders committed to improving on-demand healthcare for all.

Statement of Inclusion and Belonging
At Experity, we create an environment where every individual feels valued and respected. We believe that embracing our differences enriches our workplace, fuels collaboration, and enables us to better serve our clients and communities by living our core values of team first, lifting others up, sharing openly, setting & crushing goals, and delighting our clients. 

Experity resolves to proactively reduce the causes of workplace intolerance by facilitating awareness and education. We stand by a zero-tolerance policy against discrimination, racism or oppression in any form within our company or workplaces.

For information and resources, visit experityhealth.com.

Has $28.3M in total funding, with 4 prior funding rounds.

## About
DocuTAP is a dynamic HIT company that provides integrated technology solutions to more than 3,000 on-demand healthcare practices and primary care clinics nationwide. 

Its flagship product, DocuTAP’s EMR and Practice Management software, was created with an innovative approach to workflow. Its complete on-demand healthcare solution includes Clockwise.MD patient engagement technology, revenue cycle management services, and DocuTAP Insight—a business intelligence tool with custom reports and built-in industry benchmarks. 

We’re a fast-paced, high-growth company with a mission to improve healthcare for everyone. We focus all our energy on solutions that drive an unprecedented patient experience and provider satisfaction. We will provide comprehensive, on-demand specific solutions that enable operational excellence. 

A better on-demand healthcare experience starts with DocuTAP.

COURT PROCESS POSTED! Is taking over the site of a defunct[#urgentcare](https://www.linkedin.com/signup/cold-join?session_redirect=https://www.linkedin.com/feed/hashtag/urgentcare&trk=public_post-text)a good idea? You can get all the leasehold improvements, and possibly some furnishings, fixtures and equipment…for free. And even make a beneficial short-term, low risk deal with the landlord who at this point has little to lose.
Yes! It’s a great idea…sometimes. Depends on the answer to three questions:
--Why did the urgent care close? I ‘ve seen independent and mid-market operators backfill locations that were perfectly viable but shed by a corporate entity as part of a strategic reshuffling. These are gems. For instance, as[Optum](https://www.linkedin.com/company/optum?trk=public_post-text)started shuttering[MedExpress](https://www.linkedin.com/company/medexpress?trk=public_post-text)sites, I’m aware of a dozen successful urgent cares around the country that came in behind and successfully assumed operations. Now, if the center closed because it was a bad location, then you should run. As a rule, if you wouldn’t open a de novo here…then you shouldn’t acquire a center here.
--Related, what was the volume of the UC and can you retain it? If you can start a new UC with 20 patients per day out of the gate, you’ll be substantially ahead of the curve. If a center has been closed six months or less, and has not missed one
